sections.heading: 📜 History
sections.content_md:
Measurement is fundamental to physics — without measurement, physics is just philosophy.

**Ancient measurements** were based on human body parts: the cubit (forearm), the foot, the hand. These varied from person to person, causing confusion in trade and construction.

**The Metric System** was born during the French Revolution (1790s). Scientists defined the **metre** as one ten-millionth of the distance from the North Pole to the Equator. This was the first standardised system based on natural constants.

**Modern definitions** are even more precise:
- The **metre** is now defined by the speed of light in a vacuum (<!-- EQ_INLINE_0 --> m/s)
- The **second** is defined by the vibration of caesium-133 atoms (<!-- EQ_INLINE_1 --> oscillations)
- The **kilogram** was redefined in 2019 using Planck's constant (<!-- EQ_INLINE_2 -->)

> **Did you know?** The original "kilogram" prototype — a cylinder of platinum-iridium alloy kept in France — was the last physical artefact used to define an SI unit until 2019. It was affectionately called "Le Grand K".

sections.heading: 🌍 Nature & Applications
sections.content_md:
Measurement is everywhere:

- **📏 Length**: Measuring height, distance, width of a hair (<!-- EQ_INLINE_0 --> mm)
- **⏱️ Time**: Stopwatches for races, atomic clocks for GPS satellites
- **⚖️ Mass**: Kitchen scales, laboratory balances, weighing trucks
- **🌡️ Temperature**: Thermometers in medicine, weather, cooking
- **📊 Volume**: Measuring cylinders for liquids, displacement method for irregular solids

**Why standard units matter**: If a doctor says "take 5 mL of medicine" or an engineer says "this bolt is 10 mm", everyone in the world knows exactly what that means.

sections.heading: ⚛️ Theory
sections.content_md:
### Base SI Quantities

| Base Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|--------------|---------|--------|
| Length | metre | m |
| Mass | kilogram | kg |
| Time | second | s |
| Temperature | kelvin | K |
| Electric current | ampere | A |
| Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| Luminous intensity | candela | cd |

For IGCSE Physics, the most important are: **length (m), mass (kg), time (s), and temperature (°C or K)**.

### Prefixes

| Prefix | Symbol | Factor | Example |
|--------|--------|--------|---------|
| kilo- | k | <!-- EQ_INLINE_4 --> | 1 km = 1000 m |
| centi- | c | <!-- EQ_INLINE_5 --> | 1 cm = 0.01 m |
| milli- | m | <!-- EQ_INLINE_6 --> | 1 mm = 0.001 m |
| micro- | <!-- EQ_INLINE_7 --> | <!-- EQ_INLINE_8 --> | 1 <!-- EQ_INLINE_9 -->m = 0.000 001 m |
| nano- | n | <!-- EQ_INLINE_10 --> | 1 nm = 0.000 000 001 m |

<!-- EQ_BLOCK_0 -->

### Measuring Instruments

| Instrument | Measures | Precision | How |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----|
| Metre ruler | Length | <!-- EQ_INLINE_11 --> mm | Read at eye level to avoid parallax |
| Measuring tape | Length | <!-- EQ_INLINE_12 --> mm | For curves / long distances |
| Vernier callipers | Small lengths | <!-- EQ_INLINE_13 --> mm | Internal/external/depth measurements |
| Micrometer screw gauge | Very small lengths | <!-- EQ_INLINE_14 --> mm | Thickness of wire/sheet |
| Measuring cylinder | Volume | <!-- EQ_INLINE_15 --> mL | Read bottom of meniscus |
| Stopwatch | Time | <!-- EQ_INLINE_16 --> s | Digital or analogue |
| Top-pan balance | Mass | <!-- EQ_INLINE_17 --> g | Zero before use |
| Thermometer | Temperature | <!-- EQ_INLINE_18 --> °C | Bulb fully immersed |

### Measuring Volume of Irregular Solids

Use the **displacement method**:

<!-- EQ_BLOCK_1 -->

1. Fill a measuring cylinder with a known volume of water
2. Submerge the object completely
3. Read the new water level
4. Subtract: <!-- EQ_INLINE_19 -->

### Accuracy vs Precision

- **Accuracy**: How close a measurement is to the true value
- **Precision**: How consistent repeated measurements are

A measurement can be precise but inaccurate (e.g., a ruler that's slightly stretched).

<!-- EQ_BLOCK_2 -->

Always take **repeat readings** and calculate the average to reduce random errors.

### Uncertainty

<!-- EQ_BLOCK_3 -->

For a ruler with <!-- EQ_INLINE_20 --> mm markings, the uncertainty is <!-- EQ_INLINE_21 --> mm.
